segments.s754-g233: بفرمود is an honorific-past verb meaning 'he ordered/said' and introduces a subordinate purpose/command clause. تا introduces that subordinate clause ('so that/that'), and دخمه دیگر کنند is the clause's core: دخمه (tomb) is the object, دیگر = 'another', and کنند is the present-subjunctive/imperative form 'they should make' used after تا to express the commanded action.
segments.s754-g234: ز is the poetic preposition 'from/of' marking material; مشک is 'musk' as material. وز here functions as an archaic/poetic connector between the two materials (effectively 'and' in context). کافورش is کافور 'camphor' plus the enclitic -ش 'its', referring back to the tomb; افسر is a head-covering/helmet/crown used metaphorically for a tomb's top, and کنند repeats the subjunctive 'they should make', coordinating with the earlier کنند.
segments.s754-g235: در is the locative preposition 'in'; دخمه is the locative noun 'tomb/chamber'; شاه is the subject 'the king'. کرد here is a light/causative verb 'made/caused' used with the adjective استوار to mean 'made (himself) steadfast' or more simply 'remained/stood firm' (a common classical construction: کردن + adjective). استوار is the adjective 'steady/firm'.
segments.s754-g236: برین is the fused sequence بر + ین (بر 'on/upon' + ین as the demonstrative 'this'), here meaning 'upon/at this (place)' or 'in/on it'. بر نیامد is a negative past construction: بر (directional particle 'up/on') + نیامد 'did not come/ascend', so together 'did not get up/come out'. بسی روزگار is a temporal phrase 'many/much time' (= 'for many days'); بسی is 'much/many' and روزگار 'time/days'.
segments.s754-g237: که begins a subordinate/explanatory clause 'that/for'; شیروی is the personal name (here describing the hero, 'Shiruy'); را is the definite object marker attached to the name. زهر is the noun 'poison', and دادند is the past plural verb 'they gave/inflicted.' نیز is the adverb 'also/likewise', linking this clause to the following observation.
segments.s754-g238: جهان is the subject 'the world' and را marks it as definite/topic. ز شاهان literally 'from/because of kings' here means 'with/kindled by kings' or 'full of kings'. پرآمد is a past intransitive 'became full/overflowed' (the world became full of kings). قفیز is a rare/obscure poetic noun appended here (its precise meaning is uncertain in classical usage) and functions as a final appositive or rhetorical tag.
segments.s754-g239: به شومی is a prepositional phrase 'with/by misfortune' (expressing ill fate). بزاد is an archaic past/inchoative form 'was born' or 'came into being' (here narrative past). و is the coordinating 'and', and the second به شومی parallels the first. بمرد is a classical form of 'he died' (the stem with ب- prefixed; in context it is a plain past narrative 'died').
segments.s754-g240: همان is the demonstrative 'that same'; تخت شاهی is a genitival phrase 'throne of kingship' = 'the royal throne' (شاهی here denotes 'kingship'). پسر را places 'the son' as the definite indirect object/topic (را marks the noun as definite). سپرد is the past verb 'he entrusted/handed over' — the verb completes the clause, with the object (پسر را) positioned before it as is common in Persian.
segments.s754-g9: 'به' here is an adverbial/prepositional particle meaning 'by' or 'through' (indicating cause); 'تنبل' (adjective used adverbially) = 'laziness/sloth'; 'همی‌داشتی' is a 2nd-person singular habitual/imperfective verb meaning 'you used to keep/held' (classical form with همی-); 'شاه را' is the definite object marked by را (the king is the thing kept). Word order: adverbial phrase + verb + object.
segments.s754-g10: The clause begins with 'به' meaning 'for/to' (indicating purpose: 'for a remedy'); 'چاره' = 'remedy/solution' (object of the following action); 'فرود آوری' is a compound verb idea: 'فرود' (descent) with 'آوری' (to bring) — together 'you bring down' or 'cause to descend'; 'ماه را' is the definite object marked by را ('the moon' is what is brought down). The verb is 2nd-person singular present/habitual 'آوری'.
segments.s754-g11: 'بترس' is an imperative ('Be afraid' / 'Fear!'); 'ای گنهکار' is a vocative ('O sinner'); 'و' links clauses ('and'); 'نزد من' is a prepositional phrase 'near me / in my presence' with نزد + pronoun; 'آی' is an imperative 'come' (classical short form of آمدن). The sequence: imperative + vocative, then coordinated imperative of motion.
segments.s754-g12: 'به ایوان' is a prepositional phrase 'to/on the iwan (porch)'; 'چنین' = 'so/thus' modifying the following adjectives; 'شاد و ایمن' = 'merry and safe' describing the iwan; 'مپای' is a negative imperative (archaic classical form meaning 'do not set foot / do not step / do not go'), placed at the end to forbid the action. Overall: 'Do not go to the iwan that is so merry and secure.'
segments.s754-g13: 'برآشفت' is a past/fatḥa form meaning 'became angry' or 'was enraged' (3rd-person); 'شیرین' is a proper name (Shirin); 'ز' is the poetic preposition 'from/because of'; 'پیغام او' = 'his message' (پیغام = message, او = he/him). Word order: verb + subject + prepositional cause phrase + object pronoun.
segments.s754-g14: 'وزان' = 'and from that' (compound of و + ز آن, linking to the previous clause); 'پرگنه' = 'full of sin / sinful' describing the following action/state; 'زشت' = 'vile/ugly' qualifying 'دشنام' (insult/curse); 'دشنام او' = 'her abuse/curse directed at him' (دشنام = cursing/insult, او = him). The clause says that as a result she delivered sinful, ugly curses at him.
segments.s754-g15: 'چنین' = 'thus/so' introducing reported speech; 'گفت' is the verb 'said' (classical past), attached to 'کنکس' (the speaker) — here 'کنکس' means 'the man of the pavilion / that man'. 'که' introduces the content clause ('that/that which'); 'خون پدر' = 'the father's blood' (subject/object of the wished-for verb). Note: the entry for 'گفت' in the provided senses list does not include the verbal sense 'said', so the appropriate sense index is not available.
segments.s754-g16: 'بریزد' is a 3rd-person singular subjunctive/present used in a hortatory/wish construction here ('may ... be spilled' / 'may ... fall'); 'مباداش' (classical form of مبادا) is a negative wish particle meaning 'may it not be' and negates the verb; 'بالا' = 'up/high' and 'وبر' = 'and upon / and furthermore' — together the phrase expresses the speaker's wish that the father's blood not be spilled 'upwards' or 'over' (i.e., not to be shed in high places or widely). Word order places the verb then the negative wish particle, which is common in poetic forms.
segments.s754-g17: نبینم is a negative present/jussive form (literally 'may I not see' / 'I do not wish to see'), here first‑person singular and placed before the (optional) pronoun من for emphasis; آن بدکنش را is the definite object (را marks the object) 'that ill‑doer'; ز دور is a prepositional phrase ('from afar') with the poetic preposition ز before the adverb دور.
segments.s754-g18: نه ... نه ... is the correlative 'neither ... nor ...'; هنگام before a noun means 'at the time of ...', so هنگام ماتم = 'at the time of mourning' and هنگام سور = 'at the time of feasting' (سور here = feast). This is a terse poetic coordination of two time phrases.
segments.s754-g19: دبیری is a noun meaning 'secretaryship/office' (here 'a secretary/secretarial post') and serves as the object; بیاورد is past‑tense 'he brought' (3rd person); انده is a past participle 'placed/let be placed' forming a participial clause 'having placed'; بری is an adjective 'separate/free from' that qualifies the participle (so together 'having set (him) apart'). Word order is object + verb + participial phrase, a common classical arrangement.
segments.s754-g20: همان is the demonstrative 'that/same'; ساخته is a past participle 'made' (adjectival); پهلوی دفتری is a compound where پهلوی specifies 'Pahlavi' (the Middle Persian / Pahlavi language) and دفتری 'clerical/office' — together 'a Pahlavi clerical (office) post' or 'a Pahlavi‑trained clerk'. The phrase describes what role he was made into.
segments.s754-g21: بدان is the fused preposition+demonstrative (به + آن) 'to that'; مرد داننده is the noun 'man' followed by the participle/epithet 'knowing' (literally 'that knowing man'); اندرز کرد is the light‑verb construction 'gave advice' (اندرز 'advice' + کرد 'did/made' = 'gave counsel'). Word order places the indirect object phrase first (بدان مرد داننده) followed by the verbal predicate.
segments.s754-g22: همه خواسته is a noun phrase 'all (the) wishes/requests'; پیش او is a locative/presence phrase 'before him/in his presence' (پیش = 'in front of / in the presence of'); ارز کرد is a compound predicate where ارز (here used with کردن) means 'to present/offer or to value' and کرد is the past auxiliary — together 'he presented/offered (them)' or 'he laid all requests before him'.
segments.s754-g23: همی‌داشت is an imperfect/progressive past 'he was having / he kept'; لختی is an adverbial noun 'for a short while'; به صندوق زهر is a locative phrase 'in/at a chest of poison' (به indicating location + صندوق 'chest' modified by زهر 'poison', i.e. 'the poison locked in a chest'). The order shows the verb then duration then location.
segments.s754-g24: که introduces a causal/explanatory clause ('for/because'); زهرش is 'his poison' (زهر + possessive -ش); نبایست is the modal 'ought not / must not' (classical form); جستن is the infinitive 'to seek/search for'; به شهر is the goal/location 'in/to the city'. Together: 'for his poison ought not to be sought in the city.'
segments.s754-g177: سر بانوان بودم و فر شاه is a compact, nominative clause: سر بانوان (literally “head of the ladies”) uses the noun سر as a titular/leader sense followed by بانوان (no ezāfe written, typical in classical poetic compression). بودم is the 1st‑person past (“I was”), and the conjunction و links that predicate to the following nominal فر شاه (“the king’s far / royal glory”), so the segment states two predicates about the speaker.
segments.s754-g178: از آن پس چو پیدا شد از من گناه is a temporal/causal clause. از آن پس (literally “from that after”) = “after that,” چو = “when,” and پیدا شد is a past passive/intransitive sequence meaning “became apparent / was revealed.” از من گناه places the source/agent of the blame on the speaker (“the fault was from me / the sin was mine”).
segments.s754-g179: نباید + infinitive is an impersonal negative modal: نباید سخن هیچ گفتن = “one must not say any word.” Here سخن هیچ گفتن is an infinitival noun phrase “to say no word / utter nothing,” and بروی functions adverbially as ‘before/unto (her) / openly’ (classical phrasing), indicating the speech should not be made in her presence or openly.
segments.s754-g180: چه روی آید اندر زنی چاره جوی is a rhetorical question/explanatory clause introduced by چه (“what/what kind of”). روی = “countenance/face” here stands metaphorically for shame or appearance; آید is present/future‑oriented “comes/happens,” اندر = “in/within,” زنی = “a woman,” and چاره جوی = a compound where جوی (search/seeking) modifies چاره (“seeking a remedy”). Together: “for what face/shame comes upon a woman seeking a remedy?”
segments.s754-g181: همه یکسر از جای برخاستند is a simple past action clause: همه = “everyone/all,” یکسر here means “at once” (entirely/at one stroke), از جای = “from their places,” and برخاستند is the 3rd‑person plural past “they rose up / stood.” Word order is subject–adverbial–prepositional phrase–verb, typical in Persian narrative.
segments.s754-g182: زبانها به پاسخ بیاراستند: زبانها (“their tongues/speeches”) is the subject, به پاسخ = “for an answer / to answer,” and بیاراستند is the preterite 3rd‑person plural of بیاراستن (بی‑ + آراستن) meaning literally “they arrayed/adorned” — here idiomatically “they composed/put together (their) words in answer.” The construction uses bi‑ as an intensifier/causative prefix.
segments.s754-g183: که‌ای نامور بانوی بانوان is a vocative/relative address: که‌ای (classical vocative/relative “O / thou who art”) introduces the apostrophe; نامور = “renowned/famed,” بانوی بانوان = “lady of ladies” (superlative honorific). The sequence is a poetic address without explicit ezāfe particles but understood as epithets piled on the addressee.
segments.s754-g184: سخن‌گوی و دانا و روشن‌روان lists epithets in coordination: سخن‌گوی (literally “speech‑speaker”) functions as ‘eloquent / speaker,’ دانا = “wise,” روشن‌روان = “bright‑souled / clear‑minded.” The repeated و connects the attributes in apposition to the addressed lady; word order is adjective/noun epithets after the vocative.
segments.s754-g81: بدو گفت که ... is an older word order meaning “he said to him that ...”. بدو is the dative/enclitic-like form meaning “to him/to her”; گفت is the reporting verb “said”; که introduces the content clause; شیرویه بود این‌چنین is the reported clause “Shiruyeh was thus/like this.” Note the copula بود is past, so the whole clause is reported past tense.
segments.s754-g82: ز تیزی جوانان نگیرند کین is a causal phrase attached to the previous clause: ز here marks cause/reason (“because of / from”), تیزی جوانان is the noun phrase “the keenness/swiftness of the youths,” نگیرند is a negative present/plural verb form meaning “they do not take / they do not seize / they do not bear,” and کین is the object “grudge, hatred, vengeance.” Together: “they do not bear a grudge because of the youths' keenness.”
segments.s754-g83: چنین گفت شیرین به آزادگان = “thus said Shirin to the free/ nobles.” چنین is an adverb “thus/so,” گفت is the verb “said” (past), شیرین is the speaker’s name, and به + آزادگان means “to the free/nobles.” Word order places the manner adverb first and the indirect object after the verb.
segments.s754-g84: که بودند در گلشن شادگان is a relative clause modifying آزادگان: که = “who/that,” بودند is the past plural copula “were,” در گلشن = “in the garden,” and شادگان qualifies the garden as “joyful/the joyful ones.” The clause literally: “who were in the joyful garden.”
segments.s754-g85: چه دیدید ازمن شما از بدی is a direct question: چه ... دیدید = “what did (you) see,” with دیدید in past addressing شما (you, plural/formal). ازمن = “in/from me” specifies the locus, and از بدی = “of/because of evil” — the phrase asks “what evil did you find in me?” The word order places the question word چه at the front as usual.
segments.s754-g86: ز تاری و کژی و نابخردی is an enumerative source phrase introduced by ز (= “from/of”), listing the kinds of faults: تاری “darkness/obscurity,” کژی “crookedness/wrongness,” and نابخردی “folly/foolishness.” It completes the question by giving the putative faults the hearers might have seen.
segments.s754-g87: بسی سال بانوی ایران بدم = “For many years I was the lady of Iran.” بسی سال is an old poetic measure meaning “many years”; بانوی ایران is the titular phrase “lady (mistress) of Iran”; بدم here functions as the speaker’s past copula “I was” in older verse (archaic form).
segments.s754-g88: بهر کار پشت دلیران بدم = “for (the) work/deeds I was the support/backing of the brave.” بهر = “for/for the sake of,” کار = “deed/work,” پشت here means figuratively “back/support/protection,” دلیران = “the brave,” and the final بدم again functions as the speaker’s old past copula “I was” (i.e. I acted as their support).
segments.s754-g169: چنین here is an adverb meaning 'thus' or 'so' placed before the verb. گفت is the past tense 'said' (the provided sense list lacked the speech sense, so the actual lexical sense is 'said'). زان پس is a temporal phrase 'from that time on / then' (زان = 'from that', پس = 'after/then'). به بانگ بلند is an adverbial phrase meaning 'with a loud voice' (به marking the manner + بانگ 'voice/sound' + بلند 'loud').
segments.s754-g170: که introduces the reported content ('that' / 'that he said'). هرکس که هست is a relative clause 'whoever is' or 'whoever there is' (هرکس = 'whoever/anyone', the second که is the relative particle, هست is the copula 'is'). از شما ارجمند means 'esteemed among you' (از marking 'among/of you' and ارجمند 'esteemed').
segments.s754-g171: همه 'all/everyone' is the addressee. گوش دارید is an idiom: گوش 'ear/attention' + دارید (present tense of داشتن 'to have') = 'have ears' → 'listen' (addressing the audience). گفتار من 'my speech/words' is the direct object: گفتار 'speech' + من 'my'.
segments.s754-g172: نبیند is a negative jussive/optative form meaning 'may not see' or 'let ... not see' (negative prefix ن- + بیند 'sees'). کسی in this negative context functions as 'anyone/no one' (i.e., 'let no one'). نیز here means 'also/too' or 'even'; دیدار من 'my sight/meeting' = 'see me' or 'behold me'. The clause therefore forbids anyone from seeing the speaker.
segments.s754-g173: مگویید is the negative imperative/command 'do not say' addressed to the group (م- prefix + گوید form yields 'do not say'). یک سر is an idiomatic quantifier 'even one (bit/word)' (سر used poetically for 'a single thing/utterance'). جز از راستی means 'except/from the truth' (جز = 'except', از = 'from/of', راستی = 'truth'). Together: 'Do not say even one thing other than the truth.'
segments.s754-g174: نیاید is a jussive/optative negative 'may it not come' / 'let there not be'. ز (poetic form of از) means 'from' or 'on the part of'. دانندگان 'the knowers' = 'those knowledgeable/experts'. کاستی 'deficiency/shortcoming'. The whole clause expresses a wish that no deficiency come from the learned (i.e., let there be no lack among the knowledgeable).
segments.s754-g175: که zān پس که is a sequence of subordinators/temporal markers introducing a temporal clause: زان پس 'after that' and the following که + من + نزد + خسرو + شدم = 'when I came before/into the presence of Khosrow' (نزد = 'in the presence of', شدم = past 'I became/was' used here as 'I came to be' or 'I stood before').
segments.s754-g176: به introduces the manner/source 'from/in' here: به مشکوی زرین او نوشدم means 'I drank of his golden, musk-scented [cup/drink]'. مشکوی is an adjectival form 'musk-scented', زرین 'golden', او refers back to the king (his), and نوشدم is the past 'I drank' (or 'I took a drink').
segments.s754-g129: نمودم is a past, first-person singular verb ‘I showed/revealed’. The clause order puts the verb first (نمودم) followed by its object (همه این جادویی) and the prepositional/personal adjunct پیشت ‘before you/in your presence’. جادویی here functions as a noun-like expression ‘this magic’ (an adjectival nominalization).
segments.s754-g130: نه introduces a negation applying to the following 'از' phrase: 'not from ...'. از here means 'because of / from' (giving the cause). The sequence تنبل و مکر وز بدخویی lists causes joined by poetical conjunctions; وز is an archaic/poetic connective related to و (‘and’). The whole phrase negates the origin of the action: 'not due to laziness, deceit, or ill‑temper.'
segments.s754-g131: The negative phrase نه کس + verb yields 'no one ...'. Word order is emphatic: نه کس (no one) + موی من (my hair) + پیش ازین (before this) + دیده بود (had seen). پیش ازین is a temporal adverbial 'before this', and دیده بود is the pluperfect 'had seen'.
segments.s754-g132: This clause continues the negative: نه ... نیز بشنیده بود = 'nor had (he) heard ... either.' از introduces the source ('from the elders'), نیز adds 'also/either', and بشنیده بود is the pluperfect 'had heard'. مهتران functions as the source/people 'the elders' in context.
segments.s754-g133: ز دیدار پیران is a prepositional/ablative phrase 'from/at the sight of the elders' (ز is a poetic variant of از). فرو ماندند is a compound verb meaning literally 'they remained down' and here idiomatically 'they were abashed/overcome/kept back' — past tense, third-person plural.
segments.s754-g134: This clause has the verb برافشاندند (they scattered/they cast) as the finite verb at the end; زیر لبها ('beneath the lips') is an adverbial phrase meaning 'under their breaths' or 'murmured/under their lips'. خیو is an unusual/obscure lexical item here (probably an onomatopoeic sound or interjection); syntactically it is the object that they 'cast' beneath their lips.
segments.s754-g135: چو introduces a temporal clause here, 'when'. شیروی is an epithet 'lion‑like' (an adjective or descriptive name) modifying the (implied) subject; رخسار شیرین is the noun phrase 'the sweet/beautiful countenance'. بدید is past 'saw'. The clause therefore reads 'when the lion‑like [one] saw the sweet face.'
segments.s754-g136: روان نهانش ('his hidden soul') is the subject phrase: روان 'soul/spirit' with the possessive suffix -ش and the adjective نهان 'hidden/secret'. ز تن is a prepositional phrase 'from the body', and برپرید is a past, intransitive verb 'leapt up/rose forth'. Together: 'his hidden soul leapt from his body' — a poetic description of being overwhelmed or struck senseless.
segments.s754-g25: همی‌داشت is the habitual/continuous past form (همی- prefix + داشت) meaning 'kept/was holding'. The subject is omitted (he). آن زهر is the definite object 'that poison'. با خویشتن is a reflexive prepositional phrase 'with himself / on his person' (خویشتن emphatic), indicating possession or that he carried it with him.
segments.s754-g26: همی‌دوخت (همی- continuous + دوخت) is 'was sewing' (progressive). The direct object is marked by را: سرو چمن را = 'the meadow's cypress' (سرو = cypress; چمن modifies it). کفن is the thing being made — 'a shroud' — so the clause means 'he was sewing a shroud for the meadow's cypress'; the verb appears before the object here in poetic order.
segments.s754-g27: فرستاد is simple past 'he sent'. پاسخ is the direct object 'a reply'. به introduces the indirect object 'to' (به شیروی باز = 'to the lion‑like/fierce falcon'); شیروی is an adjectival epithet 'lion‑like / brave' and باز here means 'falcon' (an honorific/epithet).
segments.s754-g28: که‌ای is a classical vocative/introducing particle (here functioning like 'that—O' or simply the opening of the direct address). تاجور + شاه گردن‌فراز is the addressed phrase in apposition: تاجور (here 'Tajur' / literally 'merchant' in the listed senses) followed by شاه 'king' and the adjective گردن‌فراز 'proud/high‑necked'; the overall effect is a direct address, 'O Tajur, proud king.'
segments.s754-g29: سخنها 'the words' is plural; که introduces a relative clause 'that' (سخنها که گفتی تو = 'the words that you said'). گفتی here is 2nd‑person past 'you said' (classical ordering places تو after the verb for emphasis). برگست 'returned/turned back' is perfective: 'have come back'. و is the conjunction 'and', followed by باد used as an optative particle 'may...' starting the wish in the next clause.
segments.s754-g30: دل و جان is a paired nominal phrase 'heart and life/soul'. آن بدکنش is 'that ill‑doer' (آن = that; بدکنش = ill‑doer). پست is the predicate adjective 'low/base/despicable' and باد is again the optative 'may it be' (a curse/wish): 'may the heart and life of that ill‑doer be base/disgraced.'
segments.s754-g31: کجا is the interrogative 'where'. در جهان جادویی = 'in the magical/enchanted world'. جز بنام means 'except in name' or 'other than in name' (جز followed by بنام = 'except in name/only nominally'). The phrase poses an existential rhetorical question about whether a real listener exists beyond mere name.
segments.s754-g32: شنودست is a noun meaning 'a listener/hearer' (here functioning as an existence‑predicate). و is 'and'. بودست is a past existential/copular form 'there was/it was'. زان 'from that / thereof' links to the following noun شادکام 'the fortunate one' (شادکام = 'happy/fortunate'), so the clause says roughly 'and there was, from that, a fortunate one' — i.e. one truly fortunate existed for that reason.
segments.s754-g185: به یزدان is an oath phrase: به here marks the instrument/utterance 'by' (an exclamatory preposition) and یزدان is the divine addressee ('God'). که introduces a relative clause modifying یزدان ('who…'). هرگز is the negative adverb 'never', تو is the 2nd‑person pronoun topic, راکس is the direct object 'dance' (an archaic/poetic word), and ندید is the past tense verb 'saw' (here negated by هرگز).
segments.s754-g186: نه نیز begins a negative continuation 'nor/also not' (نیز reinforcing the negation). از پس پرده is a prepositional phrase 'from/behind the curtain/veil' (پس here = 'behind/after' the curtain). آوا is 'voice/sound' and شنید is the past tense 'heard'. Together: 'nor did [he] hear a voice from behind the veil.'
segments.s754-g187: همانا is an emphatic opener 'truly/indeed'. ز (poetic form of از) is the preposition 'since/from', هنگام هوشنگ = 'the time of Hushang' (هوشنگ is a proper name). باز here is an adverb 'again/ever' emphasizing continuation from that time ('ever since').
segments.s754-g188: چو is the comparative/relational 'like/as'. تو is the addressee 'you'. نیز 'also' here works with the negative verb to mean 'nor/never likewise'. ننشست is the past third‑person form 'did not sit' (negative of نشستن), and بر تخت ناز = 'upon the throne of ناز' (تخت = throne/seat; ناز = pride/indulgent luxury), i.e. 'no one like you has sat on that throne.'
segments.s754-g189: همه is the universal quantifier 'all/every'. خادمان is the plural 'servants/attendants' and پرستندگان is 'worshippers/devotees'; the conjunction و links the two nouns. The phrase is a noun-group listing people paying homage.
segments.s754-g190: جهانجوی is a compound noun/adjective 'world‑seeking' (i.e. ambitious, seeking worldly gain). و links to بیدار دل بندگان, where بیدار is 'awake/alert', دل is 'heart/mind' forming the adjective 'wakeful‑hearted', and بندگان is the plural noun 'servants/subjects' these adjectives qualify. The whole phrase further describes the assembled people.
segments.s754-g191: به آواز is an adverbial phrase 'in a voice/with a voice' → 'aloud'; به here marks the manner/instrument. گفتند is the past third‑person plural 'they said' (reported speech), کای is the vocative particle 'O', and سرفراز is an address 'exalted/proud one' (an honorific).
segments.s754-g192: ستوده is the past participle 'praised/celebrated' (predicate of the vocative). The repeated به + place names is the idiom 'in/at [place]': به چین 'in China', به روم 'in Rome' (i.e. Byzantium), and طراز here follows as a third locus or domain (ambiguous in older usage: could mean a style/realm or a place name). The series lists regions/realms where the praised reputation extends.
segments.s754-g41: 'ز گفتار چونین سخن شرم دار' is a compact, poetic clause meaning 'be ashamed of such speech.' ز (preposition) introduces the domain/reason ('because of / from'), گفتار و سخن are synonyms placed for emphasis ('speech, words'), چونین qualifies سخن ('such'), and شرم‌دار is an adjectival compound (شرم + دار) meaning 'full of shame' used predicatively; the copula/imperative sense ('be ashamed') is implied rather than explicitly stated.
segments.s754-g42: چه بندی سخن کژ بر شهریار is a rhetorical question: چه (exclamatory 'how/what') + بندی (2nd-person singular present of بستن, 'do you bind/attach' — here meaning 'do you direct/say') introduces the action; سخن کژ = 'crooked/perverse words'; بر شهریار = 'toward/against the king.' Together it means 'How dare you utter crooked words to the king?'
segments.s754-g43: ز دادار نیکی دهش یاد کن is an imperative clause meaning 'remember the Creator, the giver of goodness.' ز is the poetic preposition 'of/from' introducing the person to be remembered; دادار is 'the Creator'; نیکی دهش is a descriptive phrase ('one who gives goodness' / 'giver of goodness') where دهش denotes the act/agency of giving; یاد کن is the imperative 'remember' (یاد = remembrance; کن = do).
segments.s754-g44: به پیش کس اندر مگو این سخن = 'Do not say this speech before anyone.' به پیش کس means 'in the presence of / before anyone' (به + پیش + کس), اندر is a locative reinforcement 'in/among,' مگو is the negative imperative 'do not say,' and این سخن 'this speech/these words' is the object.
segments.s754-g45: ببردند پاسخ به نزدیک شاه is a past narrative clause: ببردند is the past/pluperfect-style form 'they took/carried' (here 'they brought'), پاسخ = 'the reply,' and به نزدیک شاه = 'to the king's near/ to the king' (i.e., into the king's presence). Word order is typical poetic inversion but literal: 'they brought the answer to near the king.'
segments.s754-g46: بر آشفت شیروی زان بیگناه: بر آشفت = 'was roused/was upset' (آشفت is past/passive-like 'became disturbed'); شیروی is an epithet ('lion-like,' here the person roused); زان = 'because of/at that' or 'from that,' linking to the preceding action; بیگناه = 'the innocent one' (object of his anger/disturbance). Together: 'the lion-like man was angered at that innocent one.'
segments.s754-g47: چنین گفت کز آمدن چاره نیست begins the reported speech: چنین گفت = 'thus he said' (چنین = 'thus/so'); کز is the conjunction 'that/for' introducing the content; آمدن = verbal noun 'the coming' (i.e. 'his coming'); چاره نیست = 'there is no remedy/solution.' The clause states there is no way to avert/prevent the coming.
segments.s754-g48: چو تو در زمانه سخن خواره نیست is a causal/explanatory clause: چو = 'when/since,' تو = 'you,' در زمانه = 'in this age/era,' سخن خواره نیست literally 'there is no 'speech-eater' (i.e. no one who swallows/accepts words),' a poetical way to say 'people no longer heed words' or 'words are not taken in this time.' The final نیست negates the existence of such persons/sentiment in the age described.
segments.s754-g57: This clause begins with the adverbial phrase 'وزان پس' (= 'from that, then'), placing the reference time before the action. 'بشیرین' is actually the preposition 'به' contracted onto the proper name 'شیرین' (classical orthography), meaning 'to Shirin'. 'فرستاد' is the simple past (3rd sg.) 'sent/dispatch(ed)' and 'کس' is the indefinite 'someone'. Word order is topical time phrase → indirect object → verb → indefinite object.
segments.s754-g58: 'که' introduces the content of the message (a reported imperative). 'برخیز' and 'پیش آی' are 2nd-person-singular imperatives: 'stand up/rise' and 'come forward/in the presence of (the king)'. The preposition sense of 'پیش' is 'in the presence of'. The final phrase 'گفتار بس' uses the noun 'گفتار' (speech) plus 'بس' used as an interjection/command 'enough' (i.e., 'stop speaking' or 'no more words'). Imperatives are linked in a simple coordinate series by 'و'.
segments.s754-g59: 'چو' is the conjunction 'when' introducing a temporal clause. 'شیرین' is the woman’s name (subject). 'شنید' is the simple past 'she heard' (learned/heard of the summons). 'آن' is the demonstrative 'that' modifying the following colors; 'کبود و سیاه' are coordinated color adjectives 'dark blue (kebud) and black', placed after the demonstrative as is common in classical Persian.
segments.s754-g60: 'بپوشید' is past (3rd sg.) of پوشیدن with the prefixed syllable 'ب' that appears in classical Persian verbs; here it means 'she put on' or 'donned'. Coordination 'و' joins the next past verb 'آمد' ('came'). 'به نزدیک شاه' is a prepositional phrase 'toward/near the king' with 'به' (to/toward), 'نزدیک' (near), and 'شاه' (king) as the object of motion.
segments.s754-g61: 'بشد' is a past/perfect-form narrative marker meaning 'it/ he became' or more idiomatically 'he made haste / became swift' in context. 'تیز' is a predicate adjective here meaning 'swift/quick'. 'تا' marks the endpoint/destination ('to/until'), and 'گلشن شادگان' is a compound place-name or poetic phrase 'the garden of the joyful'. The clause therefore expresses rapid movement toward that garden.
segments.s754-g62: 'که' is a relative particle here ('where/which'), introducing the subordinate description. 'با' in this context functions as 'at' or 'in' (indicating location/association) and links to 'جای' ('place/seat'). 'گوینده آزادگان' is a genitive construction 'the speaker (گوینده) of the آزادگان (the free, i.e. noble/free men)'; the whole phrase describes the destination's function—the seat/place of the spokesman of the free men.
segments.s754-g63: 'نشست' is the past tense verb 'sat (down)'. The prepositional complex 'از پس' (= 'from/behind') precedes 'پرده‌ای' ('a curtain', indefinite by the -ای ending). 'پادشا' (an older form of 'pādshāh') follows as the subject 'the king'—classical Persian often places the verb before its subject; here the word order is verb → locative phrase → subject.
segments.s754-g64: 'چناچون' is an adverb meaning 'thus / in that manner'. 'بود' is the past copula 'was'. 'مردم پارسا' is the nominal phrase 'the pious people' (مردم = people, پارسا = pious); the order here (adverb + copula + noun) is a poetic inversion meaning 'such was the case with the pious people' or 'the pious folk were thus.'
segments.s754-g73: وزان پس نیاسایم از پاسخت = a temporal+result clause. وزان پس (به‌آن‌پس) = 'from that time / thereafter' (وزان = به آن; پس reinforces the temporal sequence). نیاسایم is a negative perfect/past-formal meaning 'I did not rest / I did not remain at ease' (nihāsa with negative prefix نی-). از پاسخت is an oblique object/phrase 'from/for your answer' with از marking the source/thing awaited.
segments.s754-g74: ز فرمان و رای و دل فرخت = a short clause giving the cause/effect for emotional change. ز here = 'by/from' (poetic z). فرمان و رای are coordinated instruments/causes 'command and counsel'; دل فرخت is a predicative phrase 'the heart became joyful/fortunate' (فرخت used adjectivally/predicatively = 'was happy/fortunate').
segments.s754-g75: بدان گشت شیروی همداستان = 'بدان گشت' is a verbal phrase meaning 'turned to that / became so' (بدان here functions like به + آن, 'to that/in that respect'); گشت is the past of گشتن 'became/turned' used intransitively. شیروی is the subject (a proper/epic name or epithet 'the lion-like one'), and همداستان is a predicate/adjective meaning 'of one mind, like-minded, agreed'.
segments.s754-g76: که برگوید آن خوب رخ داستان = a complement clause introduced by که. Here که has a purposive/result sense ('so that' / 'that'), and برگوید is the reported verb (classical/subjunctive/infinitival sense 'to tell / may tell'); آن خوب رخ داستان is the object phrase, where خوب رخ ('fair-faced') modifies داستان ('tale/story') and آن points back to the person/event in question.
segments.s754-g77: زن مهتر از پرده آواز داد = a simple clause: 'زن مهتر' = 'the chief/notable woman' (مهتر = principal/elder/high-ranking), 'از پرده' is an ablative/prepositional phrase 'from behind the curtain' (از + پرده), and آواز داد is an idiomatic verbal phrase 'gave a voice' = 'cried out / called aloud' (داد here = gave/uttered).
segments.s754-g78: که‌ای شاه پیروز بادی و شاد = reported speech introduced immediately; که‌ای appears written together but functions to introduce the quotation plus a vocative (ای شاه = 'O king'). پیروز is an epithet 'victorious'. بادی is an archaic/optative form (here meaning 'may you be' / a wish/benediction) rather than the 'windy' sense; شاد is 'happy/joyful'.
segments.s754-g79: تو گفتی که من بد تن و جادوام = 'تو گفتی' = 'you said' (2nd-person past report). که introduces the content clause. من is the subject 'I'; بد تن is an adjectival phrase 'bad-bodied' = 'ill-bodied / unattractive in body'; و جادوام treats جادوام as a predicate 'and (I am) of sorcery / given to magic' (جادو‑ام / جادویم in older orthography). Note: the provided lemma/senses for گفتی are mismatched in the data; here it functions as 'you said'.
segments.s754-g80: ز پاکی و از راستی یک سوام = two parallel prepositional phrases of orientation/cause: ز پاکی 'by/from purity' and از راستی 'from/from truth' (both z/از marking source or affiliation). یک سوام is a short predicate: یک = 'one' and سوام (archaic) = 'I am on that side / I side with / I stand' — idiomatically 'I side with purity and truth' (i.e. I stand with them).
segments.s754-g49: چو introduces a temporal subordinate clause meaning 'when'. بشنید is 3rd-person singular past ('she heard'); in classical/poetic Persian the subject (شیرین) can follow the verb. پراز + noun (پراز درد) is a prepositional/adjectival construction 'full of pain', and شد is the copula 'became'. The whole clause = 'When Shirin heard (it), she became full of pain.'
segments.s754-g50: بپیچید is a 3rd-person singular past ('she writhed/turned/twisted'); و simply links the two actions. رنگ رخش is a noun phrase where رنگ ('color/complexion') is followed by رخ + -ش ('her face'), so 'the color/complexion of her face'. زرد شد is a predicate 'became yellow/pale' (شد = became).
segments.s754-g51: چنین داد پاسخ is literally 'thus gave an answer' (چنین = 'thus'; داد = past 'gave'; پاسخ = 'answer'). که introduces the content clause (reported speech) that follows. نزد تو = 'in your presence / to you' (نزد used as 'in the presence of'), and من is the first-person subject placed before the verb in the subordinate clause.
segments.s754-g52: نیایم here is the negative present/subjunctive form 'I will not / do not come' (classical form from نیا). مگر functions as 'except' or 'unless' and introduces the exception. با یکی انجمن is a prepositional phrase 'with one assembly/company' (یکی numerically modifies انجمن). The clause reads 'I will not come to you unless with one assembly.'
segments.s754-g53: که begins a relative/content clause 'that'. باشند is 3rd-person plural subjunctive/optative 'may be / be' expressing what should be present. پیش تو = 'before you / in your presence' (پیش used as 'in the presence of'). دانندگان is the plural noun 'knowers/experts' that is the head being described.
segments.s754-g54: جهاندیده and چیز خوانندگان are modifiers/appositions describing دانندگان: جهاندیده = 'worldly‑wise' (an epithet), و = 'and', چیز خوانندگان = 'those who are read/versed in matters' (چیز here 'matter/matter of learning', خوانندگان as 'readers/learned ones'). In older Persian compounds like 'چیز خوانندگان' function like a single descriptive phrase.
segments.s754-g55: فرستاد is 3rd-person singular past 'sent/dispatch ed'. شیروی here names the person sent (an epithet/name, 'the lion-like/brave one'); in classical Persian the agent/object of sending can follow the verb without an explicit preposition. پنجاه مرد ('fifty men') belongs to the purpose clause that follows (omission of an explicit 'to' is common in poetry).
segments.s754-g56: بیاورد is a jussive/subjunctive 3rd-singular used after the past 'sent' to express purpose: '(sent) to bring'. داننده و سالخورد are descriptive nouns in apposition to the fifty men: داننده = 'knowledgeable/expert', و = 'and', سالخورد (archaic form of سالخورده) = 'aged/elderly'.
segments.s754-g1: چو is a subordinating conjunction meaning 'when' here. آوردم is past first-person (آوردن) 'I brought/ brought about' used poetically to mean 'I reach/bring (this) day' in the narrator's account. این روز is the temporal noun phrase 'this day'. خسرو ببن is a vocative plus imperative: خسرو (Khosrow, addressed) + ببن (archaic imperative 'behold/see', older spelling of ببین), placed after the clause for poetic emphasis.
segments.s754-g2: ز is the topical preposition 'about/of' introducing the theme. شیروی و شیرین are the two names/topics (Shiruy and Shirin); شیرین here is the female name sense. گشایم is the present/subjunctive-like first-person singular of گشودن 'to open', used idiomatically 'I will open/begin' (i.e. 'I will tell'). سخن is the object 'speech/tale'. The verb گشایم precedes its object in this poetic word order.
segments.s754-g3: چو again introduces a temporal clause 'when'. پنجاه و سه is the numeral 'fifty-three' (و connects tens and units). روز is 'days'. بگذشت is past third-person singular of گذشتن used impersonally to mean '(it) passed / elapsed'. زین is an archaic fused form meaning 'from this/then' ('ز' + 'این') marking the starting point (here translated 'since'); it is a poetic/archaic temporal marker.
segments.s754-g4: که introduces the subordinate clause ('that / since'). شد here functions as an auxiliary/past marker (become/be — used to form the passive). کشته is the past participle 'killed/slain'. آن شاه is 'that king'. با is the preposition 'with'; آفرین in this line is best read as the noun/exclamation 'afarin' ('praise, acclamation' or the cry 'bravo') describing the manner or attendant cry at the event. The clause thus reads broadly as 'since that king was slain, with/under the acclamation "afarin".'
segments.s754-g5: به indicates the beneficiary/goal 'to' (to Shirin). شیرین is the addressee/name. فرستاد is the simple past third-person 'he sent'. شیروی is the agent/name (Shiruy); in this poetic order the verb appears before the subject. کس is the indefinite object 'someone' — together: 'Shiruy sent someone to Shirin.'
segments.s754-g6: که‌ای is an archaic/poetic relative phrase 'who is/that is' (که + vocative/particle). نره is an adjective meaning 'male/manly' or 'manful', applied here as a descriptor 'a manly/valiant one'. جادوی is a genitive/attributive form from جادو 'magic' (here 'magical' or 'magicianly'); in context it describes the person's magic/sorcery. بی‌دست is a compound adjective 'without hands/handless'. رس is a verbal/adjectival form of رسیدن 'to arrive/reach' used here = 'has come/arrived' (poetic, terse). Together the phrase describes 'a manly/soldierly magician who arrived without hands' (i.e. 'a manly magician who had no hands').
segments.s754-g7: همه is 'all' or 'entirely'. جادویی is 'magical / of magic' (here meaning 'magic' or 'sorcery' in general). دانی is second-person singular present of دانستن 'you know' (older form with final ی). و = 'and'. بدخویی is a noun meaning 'ill-temper / bad disposition'. The clause addresses someone: 'You know all magic and ill-temper.'
segments.s754-g8: به ایران here is locative/poetic 'in Iran' (به + place used as 'in'). گنکار is a predicate noun 'sinner' or 'wrongdoer'. ترکس is 'the Turk' (ethnic/occupational label). تویی is the archaic/poetic second-person copula 'thou art' (تویی = تو + یی). Word order places the predicate nouns before the copula for emphasis: 'In Iran, you are a sinful Turk.'
segments.s754-g89: نجستم is the 1st-person past of جستن meaning “I sought” (here: “I have sought/striven for”). The clause places the verb before the adverb (نجستم همیشه), then جز از راستی (‘except/from—only—truth’) limits the object: ‘always I sought nothing except truth.’
segments.s754-g90: This is an optative/formulaic clause: ز من دور (باد) کژی و کاستی = “May crookedness and defect be far from me.” In the manuscript the optative باد (‘may it be’) appears written as بد; here it functions as a wish. ز من = ‘from me,’ and کژی و کاستی are the things wished to be far away.
segments.s754-g91: بسی کس is an archaic quantifying phrase ‘many people’ (بسی used as a plural quantifier). به گفتار من = ‘by/through my speech’ (instrument), and شهر یافت is a past-tense clause ‘found/obtained a city’ with یافت as the past verb referring back to those people.
segments.s754-g92: ز هر گونه‌ای از جهان = ‘from every kind/type of the world’ (a prepositional phrase specifying range). بهر یافت is an idiomatic pairing: بهر (‘share/advantage/portion’) + یافت (‘obtained’) = ‘they obtained their portion’ or ‘each gained his due.’ The verb یافت again agrees with the plural subject in the prior clause.
segments.s754-g93: به ایران frames the addressee/location (‘to/for Iran’ / ‘in Iran’). که دید begins a relative clause ‘who saw’; از بنه سایه‌ام is a descriptive phrase ‘from my beneh (humble base/abode) my shade/protection.’ بنه is archaic/poetic here (sense not given exactly) and سایه‌ام = ‘my shade/protection.’
segments.s754-g94: وگر = ‘or if / or else’ introduces an alternative view; the verb دید from the previous clause is omitted by ellipsis. سایه تاج و پیرایه‌ام is a noun phrase appositive: ‘(as) the shadow of crown and my ornament’ — another way they might have seen the speaker.
segments.s754-g95: بگوید here is a 3rd-person present/optative of گفتن used to introduce reported speech (‘will say’ or ‘let him/her say’). هر آنکس که دید و شنید is a relative clause ‘every person who saw and heard’ serving as the subject of بگوید.
segments.s754-g96: همه کار = ‘all work/deeds’. ازین (از + این, here spelled ین) = ‘from this.’ پاسخ = ‘the reply/answer.’ آمد پدید is a compound verbal expression: آمد (‘came’) + پدید (‘into being/appeared’) = ‘sprang forth / came into existence.’
segments.s754-g97: بزرگان (plural noun) is the topic 'the great ones/elders'. که introduces a relative clause ('who') and باشد/بودند is the past plural copula 'were'. در پیش شاه is a locative phrase 'in the presence of the king' (در + پیش = before/in the presence of + شاه = king). Word order is topic + relative clause + locative.
segments.s754-g98: ز شیرین is a short prepositional phrase indicating relation to Shirin (here meaning 'on account of / for Shirin' or 'with respect to Shirin'), به خوبی is an adverbial phrase 'well / courteously' (به + خوبی giving manner), نمودند is the past plural 'they showed', and راه is the direct object 'the way'. The verb comes before its object (poetic word order).
segments.s754-g99: که here introduces an explanatory clause ('for / that'). چون او is a comparative 'like her' (چون = 'like/as', او = 'she'), زنی نیست is the negated existential 'there is not a woman' (زنی = 'a woman'; نیست = 'is not'), and اندر جهان gives the location 'in the world'. The order is comparative + pronoun + noun + negative verb + locative.
segments.s754-g100: The correlative construction چه ... و چه ... means 'whether ... or ...'. در آشکار = 'in the open / openly' (در + آشکار), و = 'and' linking the alternatives, اندر نهان = 'in secret' (اندر + نهان). Each part is a locative phrase contrasting public and private.
segments.s754-g101: چنین گفت شیرین is a common formula 'thus said Shirin' (چنین = 'thus', گفت = past 'said', شیرین = subject placed after the verb for emphasis). که‌ای here begins the direct speech and the combination functions as a vocative ('O'); مهتران is a plural vocative 'elders/older men/chiefs'.
segments.s754-g102: This phrase lists two adjectival descriptions applied to the addressed elders: جهان گشته (گشته = perfect/participial 'become/turned', here 'world‑wise' or 'worldly‑experienced') and کار دیده (literally 'work‑seen' = 'experienced in affairs'); و links them, and سران is the plural noun 'chiefs/leaders' (the addressees). The participle and compound adjective precede the head noun in poetic word order.
segments.s754-g103: بسه is an interjection 'Enough!' or 'That suffices.' چیز here loosely refers to 'matter/words' ('this thing'). باشد is a jussive/subjunctive/formulaic 'let there be' or 'it should be'. زنان is the direct object 'the women'. رابهی is rā + بهی (poetic/compressed), functioning as a directional object phrase 'to that place / toward it' (رَا marking the definite object and بهی = 'to it/there'). Together the segment issues the command/request 'Enough; bring the women there.'
segments.s754-g104: که introduces purpose 'so that / in order that'. باشند is the plural subjunctive 'may be / should be'. زیبای is the adjective 'beautiful', گاه is 'time/occasion' here part of the phrase گاه مهی meaning 'moon‑like occasion' or more simply 'like the moon', and مهی is an adjective 'moon‑like / lunar' used metaphorically for fairness. The clause gives the intended quality: 'so that they be beautiful, moon‑like.'
segments.s754-g105: This is a noun phrase introduced by یکی آنک 'one who/one that'. باشرم is written fused (باشرم) and functions as a prepositional phrase 'with shame/modesty' (archaic spacing), followed by و 'and' and باخواستست 'with desire/was desirous'. The sequence attributes two qualities to that person: 'with modesty and with desire'.
segments.s754-g106: که introduces a relative clause modifying 'one'. جفتش 'her/its mate' is the subject of the clause, بدو is the indirect object 'to him/unto him', and خانه آراستست is the verbal phrase. آراستست is an older/perhaps contracted compound (آراسته‌است) meaning 'has set up/arranged' — word order here is Subject (جفتش) + Indirect object (بدو) + Object (خانه) + Verb (آراستست).
segments.s754-g107: دگرآنک functions like 'another/other who' and introduces a different case; فرخ پسر is a noun phrase 'a fortunate/lucky son'. زاید is the verb 'brings forth / gives birth to' (classical third-person form) and او is the clitic pronoun referring back to the person in view. Word order is: introducer + noun phrase (فرخ پسر) + verb (زاید) + pronoun (او).
segments.s754-g108: ز is the poetic preposition 'from' (introducing source or cause). شوی here would normally denote 'husband' in older usage, but the provided lemma senses do not include that meaning (see senses mapping). خجسته is an adjective 'blessed/auspicious' modifying that noun. بیفزاید is a classical present/optative-form verb 'increases/adds (to the fortune)', and او is the pronoun receiving that increase. Grammatically this reads: 'from a blessed husband (ز شوی خجسته) he increases/adds to him (بیفزاید او)'.
segments.s754-g109: سه دیگر 'three others' begins the phrase; که introduces the relative clause. بالا و رویش pairs two nouns/adjectives 'height/loftiness (بالا) and face (روی) with the possessive clitic ش 'his/her', and بود is the past copula 'was/were'. Word order: numeral + 'other(s)' + relative clause + predicate (بود).
segments.s754-g110: به پوشیدگی is an adverbial phrase 'in/with (an attitude of) concealment/modesty' (به + noun). نیز 'also' links this clause to the previous description. مویش is 'her/his hair' (مو + clitic) and بود is again the past copula 'was' — so the clause says their hair likewise was kept concealed/modest.
segments.s754-g111: بدان گه که is a temporal phrase 'at that time when' (بدان = به + آن contracted; گه = moment/occasion; که introduces the subordinate clause). من is the subject 'I', جفت خسرو 'a mate/partner for Khosrow' is the direct object phrase placed before the verb in classical word order, and بدم here is an archaic first-person verbal form meaning 'I gave' (classical/poetic usage).
segments.s754-g112: به پیوستگی is a prepositional phrase 'into/for union/connection' (به + پیوستگی), در جهان نو is a locative adverbial 'in the new world' (در + جهان + نو), and the final بدم repeats the classical first-person verbal form 'I gave/placed' — together indicating the speaker caused/joined them in union in that (new) world. Note: several forms are archaic or poetic (پیوند/پیوستگی; the repeated b‑form of the verb).
segments.s754-g113: چو is a temporal conjunction 'when' introducing the clause. بی‌کام و بی‌دل are two adjectival phrases placed before the verb and jointly describe the (implicit) subject; بی‌کام = 'unsuccessful/unfulfilled' and بی‌دل = 'heartbroken/sad'. بیامد is the simple past 'he came'. ز روم is an adverbial prepositional phrase 'from Rum/Byzantium' following the verb. This word order (modifiers → verb → origin) is typical in epic Persian.
segments.s754-g114: نشستن here is a verbal noun/infinitive meaning 'settling, dwelling' (i.e., a place to settle). نبود is the past negative existential 'there was not'. اندرین مرز و بوم is a locative phrase 'in this border and land' (= 'in this realm'); note the existential construction places the predicate (نشستن) before the locative.
segments.s754-g115: از آن پس is a fixed temporal phrase 'from that time on / thereafter' (از = from; آن پس = that-after). بران here functions deictically/adverbially ('upon him / such') modifying کامگاری ('success/fulfillment'). رسید is the past 'arrived'/'befell' and completes the clause: 'such success arrived.'
segments.s754-g116: که introduces a relative clause describing that کامیگاری: 'that which...'. کس here is used in a negative sense with the following negative verb (کس … ندید و شنید) = 'no one'. در جهان آن = 'in that/world' ('in the world'). ندید and شنید are past-tense verbs 'saw' and 'heard (of)'; together they mean 'no one in the world had seen or even heard of it.'
segments.s754-g117: وزو here is best read as a contraction of و از او ('and from him'), a poetic/archaic sequence; the supplied lexical sense ('ablution') does not fit the context (so sense is marked -1). نیز = 'also'. فرزند بودم is a past-clause 'I was a child/son (of his)'; the numeral چهار placed after بودم gives the quantity (literally 'I was a child — four'), a poetic word order where the number follows the predicate.
segments.s754-g118: بدیشان is the pronominal prepositional phrase 'to/for them'. چنان = 'so/such'; شاد = 'joyful/happy'. بد here functions as an irregular/archaic copular or auxiliary element (classical poetry often uses short forms of 'بود' or similar), giving 'was' or marking the predication; the dictionary senses offered ('bad / ill') do not fit this syntactic role, so its sense is treated as irregular (-1). شهریار is the subject 'the king'. The phrase thus means 'the king was so joyful toward them.'
segments.s754-g119: چو at the start is a temporal/simile marker 'when / as'. نستود is an archaic past form of ستودن 'he praised/extolled' (the prosthetic n- occurs in epic forms). و چون شهریار introduces a simile 'and like a king'; فرود here is a noun ('descent, alighting') used with the preceding simile—literally 'and (in) a king-like descent'—with the subject omitted (typical of poetic ellipsis).
segments.s754-g120: چو again introduces a comparison 'as/like'. مردان here can mean 'men' or 'heroes' (epic register); شه is 'the king' (genitive relation: 'the king's men'). آن تاج چرخ کبود is a nominal phrase 'that crown of the azure sky/heavens' (تاج = crown; چرخ = sky/heaven; کبود = blue/azure). The clause is elliptical: the verb is omitted in poetry, so the noun phrase at the end is left to be understood from the preceding action.
segments.s754-g161: دگر at the start functions as 'another' (an additional item). بر + کنامی is a locative phrase: بر is the preposition 'on/at' placed before the place-name/ noun (کنامی). که introduces a relative clause modifying that place; ویران is an adjective 'ruined', and شدست is an archaic past/perfect auxiliary (poetic form of شد + است) meaning 'has become / is ruined' — together forming 'that has been ruined'.
segments.s754-g162: رباطی is the head noun 'a caravanserai / inn' and is immediately followed by a relative clause introduced by که. آرام here is adjectival 'at rest / calm' describing the lions, شیران is the plural subject 'lions', and بدست functions adverbially/agentively 'by / in the power of / as the resting‑place of' — so the relative clause yields 'a caravanserai where the lions are at rest / which is the lions' resting‑place.'
segments.s754-g163: به مزد is an adverbial/prepositional phrase 'as a reward / for reward'. جهاندار is an epithet 'world‑holder / sovereign' qualifying خسرو (apposition). بداد is an archaic past verb form used here as the main past tense verb 'gave' (poetic/older usage equivalent to داد), linking the preceding reward phrase to the implicit object.
segments.s754-g164: به نیکی is an adverbial phrase 'with kindness / kindly'. روان here is used in the formulaic sense related to departure/condition (roughly 'in good spirits' / 'sent on his way'); ورا is the unstressed pronoun 'to him / toward him' (vora = را + او/there), and کرد is the simple past of 'to do/make' used causatively 'made/sent'. شاد is the resultative adjective 'happy / glad'. The whole segment means 'he sent him off kindly / made him go away happy.'
segments.s754-g165: بیامد is the past tense 'he came'. دان here is the remnant of the demonstrative 'آن' fused with the preposition ب (rendered in the text as ب + دان = 'to that'); thus بدان باغ = 'to that garden'. و links the next clause. بگشاد is an archaic prefixed past form of 'گشود' (to open) — the prefix ب- here forms the simple past — and روی is the object 'face/countenance' (i.e. 'he opened/unveiled his face', idiomatically 'his face brightened' or 'he revealed his countenance').
segments.s754-g166: نشست is the simple past 'he sat down / settled'. از بر is a classical locative compound ('upon / on top of' or 'from on') followed by خاک 'earth / ground'. بی‌رنگ modifies خاک as 'colorless / pale' and و بوی adds 'and scent', so the full phrase describes the place he sat on: 'the pale, scentless earth.' Word order is Subject–Verb–(locative phrase) with adjectives following the noun they modify.
segments.s754-g167: همه بندگان را is the definite object 'all the servants' marked by را. بر خویش is a dative/locative phrase 'to himself / to his person' (خویش = his own), and خواند is the past tense verb 'called / summoned' (here 'خواند' = summoned). The verb takes the preceding object and the directional phrase to indicate 'called all the servants to himself.'
segments.s754-g168: مران is most likely a proper name (vocative or subject of the second clause). هر یکی 'each one' is the quantifier + noun, and رابه is the fused sequence را + به ('to/for each one'). خوبی functions adverbially 'well / with kindness / properly', and نشاند is an archaic past form meaning 'seated' (caused to sit) — so 'Maran seated each one well.' The verb is transitive and follows the object phrase as in classical Persian word order.
segments.s754-g241: Literally 'کسی پادشاهی کند هفت ماه' = 'someone reigns seven months.' 'کسی' is an indefinite subject ('someone'). 'پادشاهی کند' uses the noun پادشاهی with the verb کند (from کردن) to mean 'to rule/reign' (here present/subjunctive/optative sense often used like 'may/let someone...'). 'هفت ماه' is a duration phrase ('for seven months') placed after the verb as a temporal complement.
segments.s754-g242: 'بهشتم' here is the speaker's 'Paradise' (بهشت + possessive -م). 'ز کافور' is a poetic genitive/ablative 'of/from camphor' (ز as 'from/of'). 'یابد' is 3rd-person present of یابیدن meaning 'obtains/receives' (classical-present used to state a future/inevitable outcome). 'کلاه' is the direct object 'a hat' (used metaphorically). Word order is Subject (بهشتم) + prepositional phrase (ز کافور) + verb (یابد) + object (کلاه).
segments.s754-g243: 'به گیتی' = 'in the world' (به as a locative/prepositional particle). 'بهی بهتر' is a rhetorical doubling meaning 'nothing better' or 'the best'; بهتر is the comparative 'better.' 'از گاه نیست' uses از to introduce the standard of comparison ('than the hour'), and نیست negates the existence: 'there is not (anything) better than the time/moment.'
segments.s754-g244: This clause is parallel: 'بدی بتر از عمر کوتاه نیست' = 'there is no bad worse than a short life.' بدی ('evil, bad') is the noun, بتر is the comparative 'worse,' از introduces the comparison 'than (a) life,' عمر کوتاه is 'a short life,' and نیست negates existence — i.e. 'nothing is worse than short life.'
segments.s754-g245: 'کنون' = 'now.' 'پادشاهی شاه اردشیر' = 'the kingship of Shah Ardashir.' In classical/poetic Persian the possessive relation is here expressed by juxtaposition (پادشاهی followed by شاه اردشیر) rather than an explicit ezāfe; it means 'King Ardashir's rule/kingship.'
segments.s754-g246: 'بگویم' is the first-person present/subjunctive form 'I say / I will tell' (here used to introduce the forthcoming account). 'که' introduces the subordinate clause ('that/which'). 'پیش آمدم' literally 'came before to me' — with آمدن used impersonally it means 'befell me / happened to me earlier.' 'ناگزیر' ('inevitable/compelled') qualifies آمدم, so the clause says that the matter happened to the narrator unavoidably or that he is compelled to relate it. (Note: the provided lemma senses for بگویم in the input do not match its verbal sense here.)
segments.s754-g209: ز is a poetic preposition meaning 'because of / from' and introduces the cause (ز گفتار او = 'because of his speech'). گفتار او is a genitive phrase 'his speech/words'. ویژه is used adverbially before the predicate to intensify it ('especially/very'), and گریان is a predicative adjective/participle 'weeping' that combines with the past plural شدند ('they became') to form the verbal predication ('they became weeping' = 'they wept'). Word order places the causal prepositional phrase up front for emphasis.
segments.s754-g210: هم is a connective 'also/too' linking this clause to the previous. از introduces the cause ('from/because of'), followed by درد پرویز 'the sorrow/pain for Parviz' (درد here = emotional sorrow). بریان is a poetic predicative adjective 'burnt/consumed' used metaphorically, and شدند is the past plural copula/auxiliary 'they became / were'. The phrase as a whole expresses they were literally/figuratively 'consumed' by grief for Parviz.
segments.s754-g211: برفتند is the past plural verb 'they went / departed' (classical past form with the prefix ب-). گویندگان is the (plural) subject 'the speakers/narrators'. نزد شاه is a prepositional phrase 'to/into the presence of the king' indicating destination; the verb‑subject order (برفتند گویندگان) is common in classical poetic prose/verse.
segments.s754-g212: شنیده is a perfect participle 'having heard' used adverbially ('after they had heard' / 'having heard'). به here functions as a linking particle/ preposition attaching the participial phrase to the main verb that follows. گفتند is the main verb 'they said / they told' in the past plural. زان modifies بی‌گناه: زان بی‌گناه = 'that innocent one' (demonstrative + adjective). The clause thus means 'having heard, they told (him) about that innocent [man].'
segments.s754-g213: بپرسید is an archaic/classical past form 'he asked' (the prefix ب- appears in older Persian past forms). The following words name the addressee/title: شیروی (an epithet/name) and کای (the title 'Kay'); نیک خوی is an adjectival phrase 'of good disposition' modifying the name/title. The sequence places the verb first (typical in epic narrative), then the person addressed with his epithets.
segments.s754-g214: سه دیگر = 'three other' (سه quantifies 'دیگر'). چه چیز is the interrogative 'what thing' and آمدت is a past-form verb with the enclitic -ت 'to you' (literally 'what thing came to you' = 'what did you desire/what occurred to you'). آرزوی is 'wish/desire' and completes the question: 'What three other things do you wish?' Word order is slightly inverted for emphasis: number + 'other' precedes the interrogative clause.
segments.s754-g215: فرستاد is the past transitive verb 'sent / dispatched' (here with purposeful sense). شیرین is the subject 'Shirin' (the sender). به + شیروی is the dative/prepositional phrase 'to Shiruy' indicating the recipient of the sending. کس is an indefinite object 'someone' (i.e., 'she sent someone'). The verb precedes subject and complements, a typical narrative order.
segments.s754-g216: که introduces a subordinate content clause 'that / saying that'. اکنون is the adverb 'now'. یکی آرزو = 'one wish' (یکی = one/a single, modifying آرزو). ماند is the past verb 'remained' (a single wish remains). و بس is an idiomatic closing 'and that's enough' (i.e. 'and that suffices'). The clause reports the message the messenger carried.
segments.s754-g201: پیامی (object) + فرستاد (verb) + نزدیک من (adverbial phrase) shows a poetic word order: the message is fronted before the verb. نزدیک من functions as an indirect-object/adverbial 'to/near me' (classical Persian often uses 'نزدیک' to indicate the recipient or proximity).
segments.s754-g202: که introduces a reported-content subordinate clause 'that...'. تاریک شد is a past/resultative construction 'became dark' (شد = become/was). جان باریک من is the noun phrase 'my frail/slender life' with the adjective باریک after the noun (poetic order); من marks the possessor 'my'.
segments.s754-g203: بدان is a compacted prepositional phrase (بِ + آن) meaning 'to that person / to him', followed by گفتم 'I said'. این بد is the demonstrative 'this' + predicate 'bad/evil' (i.e. 'this ill/terrible thing'). که introduces the content clause 'that I am alive' with زنده‌ام ('I am alive') in the normal present-tense nominal form (زنده + ام enclitic = 'I am').
segments.s754-g204: جهان‌آفرین را is the definite/theme-marked object 'the Creator' (را as direct-object/emphatic marker). پرستنده‌ام is a nominal predicate 'I am a worshipper/devotee' formed from the agent noun پرستنده plus the first-person suffix ام; the phrase states the speaker's devotional stance.
segments.s754-g205: پدیدار کردم is a compound of the stem پدیدار (manifest/visible) plus کرد(م) 'I made', so 'I made manifest / revealed'. همه راه خویش is the object 'all my ways' (همه = all, راه = way/path) with خویش expressed as خوی + ش (a possessive cluster meaning 'his/own' in context 'my own' by agreement), a poetic breakdown of the possessive.
segments.s754-g206: پراز درد بودم is a stative clause 'I was full of pain' (پراز = full of, درد = pain/sorrow, بودم = I was). ز introduces cause/source 'from/because of' and ز بدخواه خویش identifies the cause as 'from my ill-wisher' or 'because of an enemy of my own' (خویش again marking the possessive/own relation).
segments.s754-g207: پس از مرگ من is the temporal phrase 'after my death' (پس از = after). بر سر انجمن is an adverbial locative/occasional phrase 'at/on the assembly' or 'before the assembly' (بر سر + noun commonly means 'in the presence of / at the head of' and here indicates the public gathering).
segments.s754-g208: زبانش 'his tongue/utterance' is the subject; مگر introduces a negative-wish/exception sense here ('lest / that not'), and بد سراید ز من is a verbal clause 'speak/sing evil of me' (سراید = may he compose/sing/say, بد = evil, ز = of/about, من = me). The whole clause is a wish/optative: 'may his tongue not speak ill of me.'
segments.s754-g145: بدو گفت شیرین is inverted word order common in classical Persian: بدو (to him) + گفت (said) + شیرین (Shirin) = “Shirin said to him.” که here is a complementizer introducing reported content; هر خواسته = ‘هر’ (indefinite/any/whatever) + خواسته (noun ‘wish’), meaning “whatever wish.”
segments.s754-g146: که بودم is a reported/clausal continuation: که (that) + بودم (1st-person past “I was” or “I had been”). بدین here is the fused classical prepositional phrase (ب + دین ≃ به این) meaning “in/at this”; کشور آراسته is a noun phrase with کشور (country/realm) and آراسته (past participle/adjective “adorned/arranged”), so بدین کشور آراسته = “adorned/arranged in this country.”
segments.s754-g147: ازین پس (از + ین(=این) + پس) = “from this time on” / “henceforth.” یکایک is an adverb meaning “one by one / each and every.” سپاری (from سپردن) here functions as an instruction/imperative/subjunctive ‘entrust’ and is followed by به من = “to me.” Word order places the adverb before the verb and the beneficiary after the verb.
segments.s754-g148: همه = “all/everyone.” پیش این نامور انجمن = prepositional phrase where پیش means “in the presence of / before,” این نامور = “this renowned,” and انجمن = “assembly/gathering”; together: “all before this renowned assembly.”
segments.s754-g149: بدین نامه = ‘by/with this letter’ (بدین = به + این, a classical fused form). اندر نهی = ‘at/in the close’ (اندر ‘in/within’; نهی here is an archaic/poetic use meaning ‘end/closing remark’ rather than the modern senses listed). خط خویش = ‘his own hand/style of writing’ where خط = handwriting/script and خوی + ش = خویش (possessive) = ‘own/his own.’
segments.s754-g150: که introduces the quoted content (“that …”). بیزارم is a first‑person present “I am averse/loathe” (here reported speech: ‘I am weary of’). از چیز او = ‘of his matter/affair’ (از = of/about, چیز = thing/matter, او = his). کم و بیش is a fixed phrase meaning ‘more or less’ or ‘to some extent.’
segments.s754-g151: بکرد آنچ فرمود = a short classical sequence: بکرد (he did / performed) + آنچ (that which) + فرمود (he ordered/commanded). شیروی is the name/epithet (literally ‘lion-like’) and زود modifies the clause as an adverb ‘quickly/promptly.’ Together: “He quickly did that which Shiroy commanded.”
segments.s754-g152: This is a temporal subordinate clause: زن (the woman) + از آرزوها (construed as ‘with regard to / concerning her wishes’) + چو (classical connective ‘when / as soon as’) + پاسخ شنود (پاسخ = answer; شنود is the classical 3rd‑person form ‘heard’). So: “when the woman heard the answer to her wishes…”
segments.s754-g33: وگر is a conditional 'if' (classical/poetic). The phrase puts the subject شاه before a descriptive prepositional phrase ازین رسم و اندازه ('of this custom and measure'), with the copula بود ('was') placed at the end as is normal in older Persian; اندازه here carries the nuance 'measure/moderation'.
segments.s754-g34: که introduces a subordinate clause ('that/so that'). رای وی is 'his counsel/judgement' with the pronoun وی following the noun (classical order). از marks source ('from/of') and جادوی تازه literally 'new magic'; بود is the final copula 'was'.
segments.s754-g35: که again introduces the clause. جادو بدی is a noun + adjective order common in poetry: 'an evil/enchanting magic' (i.e. 'an evil spell'); کس is the indefinite agent 'someone'. به مشکوی شاه marks the target with به ('upon/on the musk‑scented king'). The verb ('cast/placed') is implied/omitted, a frequent poetic compression.
segments.s754-g36: This is a compact, somewhat elliptical phrase: به دیده 'with the eye' (adverbial) followed by another adverbial/verb token به دیدی — here functioning to indicate 'by sight / as seen' (the poetic use of دیدی/دید as seeing). همان روی شاه is the object 'that same face of the king.' Note again the verb is at the end or implied; the sequence emphasizes visual perception.
segments.s754-g37: مرا is the object 'me' (here tokenized as م + را); the particle را marks the definite object. از پی is an idiomatic prepositional expression 'after / in pursuit of' (i.e. 'following'); فرخی is the proper name Farrokhi. داشتی is 2nd-person past 'you had/kept' (داشتی = you used to have/kept). Word order places the object and the prepositional phrase before the verb, typical of classical Persian.
segments.s754-g38: که introduces the explanatory clause ('for / because'). شبگیر is a role/name meaning 'night‑visitor' or 'one who keeps/watch at night'. چون is the comparative 'as/like', چشم 'eye'. بگماشتی is 2nd‑person past 'you placed/appointed' (you made/ set). The clause says why the speaker was kept: at night he was made like an eye (i.e. a night‑watcher).
segments.s754-g39: ز (poetic variant of از) indicates motive/source 'because of' or 'from'. مشکوی زرین is a descriptive compound 'musk‑scented, golden' (an epithet). مرا را is the object 'me' (tokenized here as م + را). خواستی is the 2nd‑person past 'you wanted/desired.'
segments.s754-g40: به دیدار = 'at the meeting/sight of' or 'in meeting'. من is 'me/I' as the object of دیدار. جان here means 'life/soul' and بیاراستی is a 2nd‑person past form ('you adorned/beautified' — verb at the end), so the clause means 'in meeting me you beautified (your) life' (the subject 'you' is implied).
segments.s754-g65: به نزدیک او کس فرستاد شاه is a standard SOV order with a prepositional phrase up front. به + نزدیک = 'to/near'; او is the indirect object/goal 'her/him'; کس = 'someone'; فرستاد is the past tense verb 'sent'; شاه is the subject 'the king' placed after the verb for a poetic word order.
segments.s754-g66: که introduces a subordinate explanatory clause 'for/that'. از سوک خسرو is a genitive phrase 'from/of Khosrow's mourning'; برآمد here is a past action 'arose/occurred' and ماه is 'month' (two months). The clause explains the reason for sending someone.
segments.s754-g67: کنون 'now' sets time. جفت من باش is an imperative clause: جفت = 'pair/partner', من = 'my', باش = imperative 'be'. تا introduces a purpose/result clause ('so that'). برخوری is a subjunctive/optative-like verbal form meaning 'you may obtain/receive' (poetic usage where classical forms express hoped-for outcome).
segments.s754-g68: بدان here functions as a connective roughly 'to/thereby/so that' (in older/poetic Persian formed from ب + آن); تا again marks purpose/result. سوی = 'toward', کهتری = 'inferiority/lesser (one)', and ننگری is a jussive/subjunctive form 'you should not look' (negation implied by context). The clause says 'so that you do not turn your gaze toward something inferior.'
segments.s754-g69: بدارم is a present/future-oriented first-person verbal form 'I will keep / I shall have' (classical usage expressing intention). تو را uses را as the definite object marker 'you' as object. هم بسان پدر = 'just like (as) your father' (هم = also/just, بسان = like, پدر = father/ancestor).
segments.s754-g70: وزان = 'and from that/by that' (classical connective meaning 'and thereby'); نیز = 'also/too'; نامی‌تر and خوب‌تر are comparative adjectival forms 'more famous' and 'better/more handsome' respectively, describing the benefit/result of the previous promise.
segments.s754-g71: بدو = 'to him' (dative). The expected verb 'گفت' here is the past 'said' (poetic past of گفتن), but the supplied sense list did not include the simple past; grammatically this is a reporting clause 'Shirin said to him that...'. که دادم نخست = 'that I gave first' or 'what I gave at the beginning' where دادم is past 'I gave' and نخست = 'first/initially'.
segments.s754-g72: بده is the imperative 'give!' (an order). وانگهی = 'thereupon/after that'. جان من پیش تست is an idiomatic classical phrase: جان من = 'my life' (meaning the speaker's person/self), پیش = 'before/in the presence of' and تست is an archaic 2nd-person copula 'you are' (so 'my life is at your disposal / before you'). The clause promises that after the giving, her life will be under his control/possession.
segments.s754-g137: ورا گفت is a two-word reporting phrase: ورا (prepositional/oblique pronoun) = 'to him/to her', followed by گفت, the past-tense verb 'said' (3rd singular). The remainder جز تو نباید کسم is a negated modal construction: جز تو 'except you' + نباید as an impersonal/modal negative ('should not / there is no need') with کسم ('my person' = 'any other person for me') as the thing negated. Word order (object/oblique before verb) is normal in classical Persian narrative.
segments.s754-g138: چو here functions as a conditional 'if/when' and governs the subjunctive یابم. تو is the conditional subject 'you', جفت is the direct object 'a mate/spouse', and یابم is 1st-person subjunctive/optative 'may I find / I should find'. به ایران is an adverbial phrase pointing to the place ('in/to Iran'), and بسم (بس) at the end is an archaic predicate meaning 'it is enough / suffices' — a short, emphatic conclusion.
segments.s754-g139: زن خوب رخ is a nominal phrase 'the woman of fair/beautiful face' (خوب modifying رخ). پاسخش is 'her/his answer' (پاسخ + enclitic pronoun ش), and داد is the past verb 'gave' used idiomatically to mean 'gave (her) answer' = 'replied'. باز is an adverb 'again', placed after the verb to indicate repetition.
segments.s754-g140: که introduces reported speech ('that ...'). از شاه ایران is a prepositional phrase 'from/of the king of Iran' indicating source/authority. نیم here is an archaic/contracted negative copula meaning 'I am not' (first-person negative, common in older Persian), followed by بی‌نیاز 'free from need / independent' as the predicate. Together it yields 'that I am not free/independent of the king of Iran.'
segments.s754-g141: سه حاجت (three requests) is the direct object placed before the verb, a common order in Persian. بخواهم is 1st-person present-subjunctive/optative of خواستن and here expresses a future/volitional sense 'I will ask' or 'I shall request'. چو فرمان دهی is a temporal/conditional clause 'when/if you give the command' with دهی in the 2nd-person subjunctive/present.
segments.s754-g142: که introduces the purpose/result clause ('that ...'). بر تو is a prepositional phrase literally 'upon you' used idiomatically here to mean 'with you' or 'to you'. بماناد is a subjunctive/optative form 'may remain' (old/stylistic morphology), and شاهنشهی = 'the kingship / imperial rule' is the thing that is wished to remain with you.
segments.s754-g143: بدو گفت is another reporting phrase: بدو 'to him/unto him' followed by گفت 'said' (past). شیروی جانم is a vocative address: the name or epithet شیروی followed by جانم ('my life' = 'my dear') where جانم contains the possessive suffix. توراست is a short predicate 'you are right / it is as you say', closing the addressed remark.
segments.s754-g144: دگر آرزو 'as for another/any other wish' (دگر = 'other'), each of the next two words forms a relative clause: هرچ 'whatever' + خواهی 'you wish' (2nd-person present). رواست is the predicate 'is permitted / is fitting' (روا + copula ست), giving the meaning 'whatever else you want is permissible.'
segments.s754-g193: که (that) introduces the subordinate/rhetorical clause. یارد (yār) is the agent 'friend/companion' placed before the verbal noun phrase; سخن گفتن is a compound infinitive 'to speak/talking' used as the action attributed to یارد. از تو (about you) follows سخن گفتن to mark the topic 'concerning you', and به + بد makes an adverbial 'badly/ill' (به as an adverbial prefix + بد 'poorly').
segments.s754-g194: بدی کردن is a light‑verb construction: بدی (harm/evil) + کردن 'to do' = 'to do harm'. از رویِ تو is a prepositional phrase meaning 'on account of you / because of your (face/appearance)', where روی is 'face/appearance'. کی سزد is a rhetorical question: کی 'when' and سزد (impersonal present form of سَزد 'befit') meaning 'when would that be fitting?'.
segments.s754-g195: چنین 'thus/so' modifies the reported speech; گفت is the verb 'said' in past tense (here introducing reported speech) and شیرین is the speaker's name following the verb — a common poetic word order (verb before subject). که introduces the following subordinate clause, and این بدکنش 'this ill‑doer' is the topical noun phrase being spoken about.
segments.s754-g196: که 'that/which' begins the relative/result clause. چرخ بلند is a poetic compound 'the lofty sky/heavens' (چرخ = heaven/fate, بلند = lofty). The clitic ش (= 'him') refers back to بدکنش, and کند سرزنش is a verbal phrase where کند (present/optative 'may/might do') + سرزنش 'reproach' together mean 'may the heavens reproach him' (کند expressing optative/potential in reported speech).
segments.s754-g197: پدر را is the definite object 'the father' (را marks it). بکشت is an archaic/past verbal form meaning 'he killed' (past action). از پی is a prepositional phrase 'in pursuit of / after' and is followed by the coordinated goal تاج و تخت 'crown and throne'.
segments.s754-g198: کزین پس 'from this/after this' sets the temporal/resultive frame. مبیناد is a present/formal poetic verb 'is seen/appears' used here prophetically (often implying 'will no longer be seen' in context). شادی و بخت 'joy and fortune' are the subjects of مبیناد linked by و.
segments.s754-g199: مگر here introduces an exception/contrast ('except/except that' or 'but'), followed by مرگ را where death is grammaticalized as the object (a personified force). پیش دیوار is a prepositional/adverbial phrase 'before/in front of a wall', and کرد is the past tense verb 'did/caused/placed'. The construction personifies death as something 'made to happen'.
segments.s754-g200: که introduces the result clause: جان پدر را 'the father's life' (marked by را) is the direct object, and به تن ('to/for the body') indicates the sphere or relation in which that life became diminished. خوار کرد is a causative/resultative phrase 'made (it) despised/lowly' where خوار is the predicate adjective and کرد is the light verb 'made/did'.
segments.s754-g225: هم آنگه is a two-word temporal phrase: هم (emphatic 'also/right then') + آنگه ('then'). زهر هلاهل is a noun phrase with زهر ('poison') modified by هلاهل (a poetic/compound name or epithet here, literally 'halāhel' — a jingling‑ornament word used as a named poison or figurative substance). بخورد is the narrative past/preterite form of خوردن used here to mean 'she swallowed/ingested'.
segments.s754-g226: ز شیرین is a prepositional phrase 'from Shirin' (ز = 'from', شیرین = the female name Shirin). روانش ('her soul') is the direct noun (روان + possessive suffix -ش). برآورد is a past verb here meaning 'brought out' or 'caused to come forth', and گرد is an archaic/co-textual auxiliary akin to گشت ('became/was'), so the clause says her soul was brought out from Shirin.
