segments.s754-g227: نشسته is a past participle 'seated' used predicatively. بر شاه here means 'before/on the presence of the king' (بر = 'on/upon/before', شاه = 'the king'). پوشیده روی is a noun phrase 'with face covered' (پوشیده = 'covered', روی = 'face'). Word order is typical poetic: participle + prepositional phrase + adjectival phrase describing posture/appearance.
segments.s754-g228: به تن is a locative phrase 'upon her body' (به = 'on/to', تن = 'body'). بریکی جامه is a noun phrase 'a delicate (narrow/dainty) garment' where بریکی qualifies جامه. کافور بوی is a postpositive compound meaning 'camphor‑scented' or 'with the scent of camphor' (کافور = camphor, بوی = scent). Adjectives and scent-nouns follow classical Persian poetic order.
segments.s754-g229: به دیوار پشتش نهاد is built around نهاد (past of نهادن 'to place/put'): به دیوار ('against the wall'), پشتش ('her back' = پشت + -ش), نهاد = 'he placed/laid (her)'. و بمرد follows as a short narrative clause; بمرد here functions as the narrative past 'died' (classical narrative form).
segments.s754-g230: The second clause repeats بمرد ('he died') for emphasis. ز گیتی نشانش ببرد = 'and (he) took/removed her sign/trace from the world': ز گیتی ('from the world'), نشانش ('her trace/mark' = نشان + -ش), ببرد is the present/past narrative form of بردن used here to mean 'took away/removed'. The sequence is compressed, typical of epic narrative.
segments.s754-g231: چو بشنید is a temporal subordinate ('when he heard'); چو = 'when', بشنید = past 'he heard'. شیروی is an adjectival epithet 'lion-like' or 'brave' modifying an implied person. بیمار گشت = 'became sick' (بیمار = 'sick', گشت = 'became'). The clause describes a change of state upon hearing the news.
segments.s754-g232: ز دیدار او پر ز تیمار گشت is causal/resultive: ز دیدار او = 'from/at seeing her' (ز = 'from/because of', دیدار = 'sighting/meeting', او = 'her'), پر ز تیمار = 'filled with care/solicitude' (پر = 'full (of)', تیمار = 'care/treatment/concern'), and گشت again is 'became'. Together: 'on seeing her he became filled with care.'
segments.s754-g121: This prepositional/relational phrase begins with ز (a poetic 'from/of'), naming Jam and Fereydun as the source. چو here means 'as' or 'like', and نزاد is the classical past/stative meaning 'was begotten / was born' (from زاد). The sequence literally reads 'from Jam and Fereydun, as they were born' — i.e., 'like those born of Jam and Fereydun.'
segments.s754-g122: زبانم 'my tongue/speech' is the grammatical subject/topic. مباد is an optative/negative jussive meaning 'may it not be' (a classical wish). ار introduces a conditional 'if/should', and بپیچم is the 1st-person present/jussive form 'I twist/turn' (to twist one's tongue). ز here is the poetic 'from', and داد means 'justice' (not the verb 'gave'). Together the clause reads: 'May my tongue not be (so), if I twist it away from justice.'
segments.s754-g123: بگفت is a classical past form 'he said' (the b- prefix appears in narrative past). و joins the clauses. بگشاد here functions as the past verb 'opened / drew back' (the lexeme گشاد/گشود used as action), followed by چادر 'veil/cloth' as the object and ز روی 'from (the) face' indicating from whose face the veil was removed.
segments.s754-g124: همه is the universal quantifier 'all/every'. The phrase روی ماه is an idiomatic simile 'face like the moon' (moon = beauty), and the parallel همه پشت موی literally 'every back/nape [was] hair' — i.e., 'every nape like a mass of hair.' The structure is parallelism: 'every face [like the] moon and every nape [a heap of] hair.'
segments.s754-g125: سه دیگر 'three other(s)' is a numeric + adjective phrase. چنين است is the predicative 'is thus/so'. رویم here refers to 'my appearance / my face shown' (an idiomatic use of روی as 'appearance'); که هست is a relative/explanatory tag 'that are' or 'as are'. The whole segment sets up that 'three other [features] of my appearance are as follows.'
segments.s754-g126: یکی‌گر is a conditional 'if one (of them)...' ( = 'if any one'). دروغست is a nominal predicate 'is false / is a lie'. بنمای is an optative/imperative form meaning 'reveal! / show it!' (from نِماییدن with b-). دست is the instrumental 'hand' (here 'by/with your hand' indicating demonstration). The clause challenges the listener: 'If any is false, show it with your hand.'
segments.s754-g127: This segment contains an object phrase starting with مرا ('me') — note the edition splits مرا into 'م' + 'را'; grammatically this is the object 'me' (را is the definite/object marker). از introduces the source/topic 'from/of', هنر 'the art/skill' modifies موی 'hair' (i.e., 'the art of hair'), بد qualifies موی as 'bad' or could be intensifying, and در نهان is an adverbial 'in secret / privately'. Syntactically this yields 'do not keep from me, in secret, the art of hair...' (elliptical verb of forbidding or request understood from context).
segments.s754-g128: که introduces an explanatory/subordinate clause 'for/that'. آن is a demonstrative 'that'. راندیدی is the 2nd-person past 'you drove/expelled' (here part of a perfective observation), کس is 'anyone/someone', اندر جهان is the locative phrase 'in the world'. The clause reads roughly 'for you have driven/seen no one in the world to/for that' — i.e. 'you have not seen anyone in the world who has done that.'
segments.s754-g217: گشایم is the 1st-person singular verbal form (classical/poetic) of گشودن used here as a simple past-like narrative 'I opened'. در is the noun 'door' (not the preposition) governing دخمه in a construct: 'درِ دخمهِ شاه' = 'the door of the king's tomb'. باز functions adjectivally/adverbially with باز کردن (here part of the verbal idea 'to open'). Word order in poetry permits the verb before its object phrase.
segments.s754-g218: به دیدار is a purpose phrase 'to (a) visit / to see'; آمدستم is the 1st-person past/preterite 'I came / I approached' (poetic form), placed before the noun need/intent; نیاز is a noun meaning 'supplication/entreaty' and serves as the content of the visit ('I came to see him in supplication'). The verb appears after the phrase marking purpose, a common Persian order.
segments.s754-g219: چنین گفت شیروی = 'thus said Shiruy' — the verb گفت (past of گفتن) appears before the agent (inverted order typical in verse). کاین is the fused relativizer/pointing 'that/which this', هم = 'also', and رواست (روا + ست) is the predicate 'is fitting/appropriate' (ست is the copula).
segments.s754-g220: بدیدار here functions as a prepositional/purpose phrase 'to/for a visit' (equivalent to به دیدار). آن مهتر = 'that elder/chief'; او is the third‑person pronoun 'him' or 'he'; پادشاست is a nominal predicate 'is king' (پادشاه + است, contracted). Together the clause says that visiting that elder is (for him) like meeting the sovereign.
segments.s754-g221: نگهبان is the subject 'the guard'. در is the noun 'door' forming درِ دخمه 'the door of the tomb'; را is the definite object marker attaching to the whole object phrase. باز کرد is the compound verbal idea 'opened' (باز as adjective/part of 'باز کردن' + کرد as light verb). The clause follows subject–object–verb order.
segments.s754-g222: زن پارسا = 'the pious woman' (modifier after noun, adjectival/parsa). مویه 'lament' is the verbal noun/object and آغاز کرد is a periphrastic 'began' (آغاز + کرد = 'began to ...' or 'set about ...'). Thus she started lamenting.
segments.s754-g223: بشد is an impersonal/classical past-like verb 'it happened / came to be' (here narrating a change of state). چهر بر چهر is a reduplicated phrase 'face upon face' (faces touching), and نهاد is the past of نهادن 'placed' — together conveying '(her) face lay upon Khosrow's face' with the agent omitted but understood from the prior clause.
segments.s754-g224: گذشته سخنها = 'the past words' (سخنها = plural 'words/talks'); برو کرد یاد is an idiomatic/periphrastic verbal cluster where برو (here adverbial 'forth/away') combines with کرد ('did') and یاد ('remembrance') to mean 'brought to mind / recalled'. The whole segment says she recalled the words of the past.
segments.s754-g153: به راه آمد is a poetic/inverted word order where the verb آمد (simple past 'came/set out') follows the prepositional phrase; literally 'to/onto the road came' = 'set out on the road'. از گلشن شادگان is the source phrase 'from the garden (of) the joyful' — گلشن = 'garden' and شادگان (plural) is the people who are joyful and functions as the implied subject of آمد (poetic inversion allows the verb to appear before its subject).
segments.s754-g154: ز پیش here means 'in front of / before / in the presence of' and introduces the people before whom the action happens. بزرگان و آزادگان are the coordinated nouns 'the great (nobles)' and 'the free (freemen)'; the preposition z governs the whole phrase.
segments.s754-g155: به خانه شد uses شدن + به + noun to mean 'went/returned home' (classical idiom: 'became to the house' = 'went home'); شد is past of شدن used idiomatically for movement. و بنده آزاد کرد is a coordinated clause: object بنده (servant/slave) modified by the adjective آزاد (free) appears before the transitive past verb کرد 'made/caused' (= 'freed').
segments.s754-g156: بدان خواسته is a classical fused expression (بدان = به + آن) meaning 'according to/with that (request/wish)'; خواسته is the noun 'wish/request'. بنده را شاد کرد is a standard object-before-verb order: بنده را (the servant, marked by را as definite object) + شاد کرد ('made [him] happy').
segments.s754-g157: دگر here means 'then/next' (a temporal/sequence adverb). هرچ بودش is a compact phrase 'whatever was his' (هرچ = 'whatever'; بودش = بود + enclitic ش 'was his' = 'he had'). به درویش داد is 'gave to the dervish' with the dative preposition به and the past verb داد 'gave'.
segments.s754-g158: This is a relative/recipient clause introduced by the classical pointer means 'to that one who ...': بدان (به + آن) 'to that', کو functions as a relative 'who/which' in older usage, and ورا is the dative 'to him'. خویش (here written as خوی + ش) means 'his own' (possession). بیش means 'more' and داد is the past verb 'gave'; the whole says roughly 'to the one who had given him more than his own, he gave still more.' Grammatically, the clause sequences pointer (بدان) + relative (کو ...) + indirect object (ورا) + possessed noun (خویش) + quantifier (بیش) + verb (داد).
segments.s754-g159: ببخشید is past of بخشیدن used here in the sense 'bestowed/gave (alms or offerings)'. چندی is a quantity adverb 'some' and محبتِ به آتشکده marks the indirect object or place 'to/at the fire-temple' (به + آتشکده).
segments.s754-g160: This short clause lists the occasion/reasons: چه برجای و روز و جشن سده — برجای here means 'on the spot/for the occasion' or 'in honour of the place/occasion', روز = 'day', جشن = 'celebration/festival', and سده refers to the Sadeh festival (not 'century' in this context). The sequence names the occasion(s) for the offerings.

sentences.s754-s117: He ordered that they make another tomb and that they fashion its crown/cover from musk and camphor.

sentences.s754-s92: ‘O famed lady of ladies, speaker, wise and bright‑souled,’

sentences.s754-s42: Shirin spoke thus to the free (nobles) who were in the joyful garden.

sentences.s754-s44: For many years I was the lady of Iran; for deeds I was the backing of the brave.

sentences.s754-s86: All of you, give ear to my words; let no one see (or behold) me.
sentences.s754-s87: Do not speak even a single thing except the truth; let there be no deficiency among the knowledgeable.
sentences.s754-s88: For after that, when I came into the presence of Khosrow, I drank from his golden musk-scented cup.
sentences.s754-s65: I showed you all this magic; it was not born of laziness, deceit, or ill temper.

sentences.s754-s67: They were struck down/overcome at the sight of the elders; they breathed a sound under their lips.
sentences.s754-s68: When the lion‑like one saw the sweet countenance, his hidden soul leapt from his body.
sentences.s754-s13: He kept that poison with himself, and was sewing a shroud for the cypress of the meadow.

sentences.s754-s15: The words you spoke have turned back, and may the heart and life of that ill‑doer be low (be disgraced).
sentences.s754-s16: Where in the enchanted world, except in name, is there a hearer? And there was, for that, a fortunate one.

sentences.s754-s94: Truly — since the time of Hushang — never again has one like you sat upon the throne of pride.

sentences.s754-s96: They said aloud, 'O exalted one, praised in China and in Rome and in style.'
sentences.s754-s21: Be ashamed of speaking such words — how dare you direct crooked words at the king?
sentences.s754-s22: Remember the Creator, the giver of goodness; do not say this speech before anyone.
sentences.s754-s23: They carried the reply to the king’s presence; on that the lion-like (heroic) one was disturbed at that innocent man.
sentences.s754-s24: Thus he said: 'There is no remedy for his coming; in this age, words are not swallowed (no one heeds such speech).'
